learn.title: India's Green Hydrogen Mission Overview
learn.intro: Hydrogen produced with renewable power can replace fossil fuels in industry, transport and power. India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission sets ambitious targets and financial incentives to jump‑start this clean fuel, positioning the country as a future exporter while cutting emissions.
learn.c1_text: The mission aims for 5 million metric tonnes of green hydrogen annually by 2030 – roughly the amount needed to power 30 million cars for a year. This volume would require about 30 GW of renewable capacity dedicated to electrolysis.
learn.c2_text: Producers receive a capital subsidy up to 30 % of project cost and a production‑linked incentive of INR 4 kWh for each kilogram of green hydrogen, making projects financially viable against grey hydrogen prices.
learn.c3_text: The mission is backed by the MNRE’s 2023‑2027 Renewable Energy Programme, which already supports 140 GW of solar and 60 GW of wind, creating the renewable base needed for large‑scale electrolysis.
learn.stat1: India aims for 5 million tonnes green hydrogen per year by 2030
learn.stat2: Capital subsidy up to 30 % of electrolyzer project cost
learn.stat3: Production incentive: INR 4 kWh per kg H₂
learn.stat4: Required renewable capacity: ~30 GW for electrolysis

learn.title: Rooftop Solar & Net Metering in India
learn.intro: Rooftop solar lets households and businesses generate clean power on‑site, reducing grid load and electricity bills. India’s net‑metering policy ensures surplus energy is fed back to the grid, creating a win‑win for consumers and utilities.
learn.c1_text: Photovoltaic modules convert sunlight into DC electricity. A typical 5 kW rooftop system can produce about 6,500 kWh per year – enough to run a 3‑bedroom home and save roughly INR 70,000 in bills annually.
learn.c2_text: When a rooftop system generates more than the home uses, the excess is exported to the grid and credited at the same tariff as consumption. The meter runs backward, effectively lowering the next month’s bill.
learn.c3_text: State electricity regulatory commissions adopt a uniform 1:1 credit ratio. As of 2024, over 1.5 million rooftop installations (≈ 12 GW) are connected, driven by MNRE’s subsidy of up to 30 % for residential projects.
learn.stat3: Annual generation per 5 kW system: ~6,500 kWh
learn.stat4: MNRE subsidy: up to 30 % for residential PV

learn.title: Wind Turbine Lifespan & Blade Recycling
learn.intro: Wind turbines deliver clean power for decades, but their massive blades pose end‑of‑life challenges. Understanding lifespan, maintenance, and emerging recycling methods is key to making wind truly sustainable.
learn.c1_text: Modern turbines are designed for 20‑25 years of operation. With proper maintenance, many exceed 30 years, delivering over 100 GWh per turbine before retirement.
learn.c2_text: Routine inspections, blade cleaning and gearbox oil changes keep availability above 95 %. Predictive monitoring using SCADA data can cut unplanned downtime by 15 % and extend life by 2‑3 years.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2023‑2027 wind plan includes a pilot in Gujarat to convert composite blades into cement additive, targeting 10 % of de‑commissioned blades by 2028 and creating jobs in the circular economy.
learn.stat4: Average energy output: >100 GWh per turbine lifetime

learn.title: Carbon Markets & Clean Energy Incentives
learn.intro: Carbon markets assign a price to greenhouse‑gas emissions, rewarding projects that cut or avoid CO₂. By generating tradable credits, clean‑energy developers can access new financing streams and accelerate decarbonisation.
learn.c1_text: One credit equals one tonne of CO₂ avoided. Projects earn credits by replacing fossil generation with renewable power, then sell them on compliance or voluntary markets, often fetching $20‑$50 per credit.
learn.c2_text: Renewable generators register under standards like Gold Standard or Verra. Verified emissions reductions are quantified using baseline models, ensuring each credit reflects real, additional climate benefit.
learn.c3_text: India’s Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme has awarded over 2 billion INR in carbon credits to energy‑intensive units. The upcoming India‑EU carbon border adjustment mechanism will further incentivise export‑oriented clean‑energy projects.
learn.stat1: Carbon credit price range: $20‑$50 per tonne
learn.stat2: India PAT scheme: >2 billion INR in credits awarded
learn.stat3: Global voluntary market volume 2023: ~ 380 Mt CO₂e
learn.stat4: Potential revenue boost: up to 15 % for solar farms with credits

learn.title: Photoelectric Effect in Solar PV Panels
learn.intro: Solar photovoltaic (PV) panels turn sunlight directly into electricity through the photoelectric effect. Grasping this core physics helps learners appreciate why sunshine powers homes and why panel efficiency matters.
learn.c1_text: When photons strike silicon cells, their energy knocks electrons loose from atoms, creating electron‑hole pairs. Each photon above 1.1 eV (silicon bandgap) can generate one charge carrier, similar to a pool ball striking and freeing a floating ball.
learn.c2_text: Built‑in electric fields at the p‑n junction push electrons toward the front contact and holes to the back, creating a flow of DC electricity when the circuit is closed. Typical 1 m² panel yields about 150‑200 W under standard test conditions.
learn.c3_text: The MNRE’s 2024 Solar Mission targets an average module efficiency of 22 % for new installations, up from 18 % in 2020, to boost energy yield on limited rooftop space across dense Indian cities.
learn.stat2: Typical panel output: 150‑200 W per m²

learn.title: The Duck Curve Challenge for Grids
learn.intro: High solar penetration flattens midday demand but creates a steep ramp‑up in the evening as the sun sets. This “duck curve” stresses conventional generators and requires smarter grid management to keep lights on.
learn.c1_text: Midday solar output pushes net load down, forming the duck’s belly. As solar wanes, demand spikes sharply – the neck – requiring rapid ramp‑up of gas or storage to avoid blackouts.
learn.c2_text: Flexible resources like battery storage, demand‑response, and fast‑start gas turbines can fill the gap. Each MW of battery can shave up to 30 MW of ramp need, smoothing the curve.
learn.c3_text: In Delhi‑NCR, 10 GW of solar created a 45 % dip in midday load (2023). The state added 2 GW of pumped hydro and 1 GW of battery storage to curb the evening ramp, reducing curtailment from 12 % to 5 %.
learn.stat1: Typical duck‑curve midday dip: 30‑50 % of peak load
learn.stat2: Battery can reduce ramp rate by up to 30 MW per MW installed
learn.stat4: Curtailment reduced from 12 % to 5 % with storage
